problem.title: Unique Paths II
problem.description: You are given an m x n integer array obstacleGrid. There is a robot initially located at the top-left corner (i.e., obstacleGrid[0][0]).

example.explanation: Center cell is an obstacle. Two paths remain: top-right corner and bottom-left corner routes.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Dynamic Programming [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: The grid is processed in-place: obstacle cells are zeroed out, while free cells accumulate the number of paths reaching them from the top and left neighbors. Initializing the first row and column to 1 (until an obstacle blocks further propagation) sets the base cases correctly. The key insight is that an obstacle irreversibly sets a cell's path count to 0, which automatically prevents all downstream cells from counting paths through that obstacle.

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Space-Optimized 1D DP [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Use a single 1D array dp of length n (number of columns). Initialize dp[0] = 1 if obstacleGrid[0][0] == 0, else 0. For each row, update left to right: if obstacle, set dp[j] = 0; otherwise dp[j] += dp[j-1]. This accumulates paths from above (dp[j] before update) and from the left (dp[j-1] after update). O(m*n) time O(n) space.
